claim: src/remote-client/prebuilt.ts duplicates ssh-command.ts's injection-safety validation verbatim: SAFE_REMOTE_PATH = /^[A-Za-z0-9._/~-]+$/ and SAFE_NONCE = /^[A-Za-z0-9]+$/ (identical literals) plus checkAgentDir/checkNonce re-implement checkRemotePath's exact semantics, instead of importing from ssh-command.ts. The stated rationale in the file header ('so this module stays a single, self-contained, dependency-free pure module') doesn't hold in practice: prebuilt.ts is only ever consumed by bootstrap.ts, which already imports ssh-command.ts directly, so no actual decoupling is achieved -- there are now two independently-maintained copies of the security-sensitive remote-path charset.
fix: Export checkRemotePath (and the nonce check) from ssh-command.ts (or a small shared validation module) and import them from prebuilt.ts instead of re-declaring the regexes and validators. If purity/no-import is a hard constraint, add a structural test asserting the two charset regexes stay textually identical so drift is caught mechanically instead of by convention.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: bootstrap.ts nearly doubled in size (353 -> 673 lines) by bolting the entire node-pty co-provisioning orchestration (runNodePtyProbe, runNodePtyInstall, coProvisionNodePty, readLocalManifest, walkBundleDir, ~330 lines) onto the existing agent-bootstrap module, rather than extracting it into its own file (e.g. node-pty-provision.ts) that bootstrap.ts merely calls. Within that growth, runNodePtyProbe and runNodePtyInstall each re-implement, almost line-for-line, the same spawn+abort-listener+settle-once+kill/destroy-streams-on-failure boilerplate already present twice in the file (connectAgent, provisionAgent) -- the pattern is now duplicated 4x with no shared helper, so a future fix to the abort/teardown logic (e.g. another FINDING-001-class bug) requires patching all four copies correctly.
fix: Extract the common 'spawn an ssh exec child, wire an AbortSignal listener, settle exactly once, tear down streams on any non-ok outcome' shape into one parameterized helper (taking argv, an outcome classifier, and an abort-diagnostic factory) used by all four call sites; and move the node-pty co-provisioning gate into its own module under src/remote-client/ so bootstrap.ts stays focused on the F19 connect/provision-once flow.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: coProvisionNodePty (bootstrap.ts) narrows decideNodePtyProvision's 4-variant ProvisionDecision result to just 'skip' vs (implicitly) 'install' with a comment ('selection.ok narrows decideNodePtyProvision to skip|install only') instead of an exhaustive switch or a defensive assert. TypeScript does not actually enforce this cross-function invariant -- decideNodePtyProvision's return type still includes 'proceed-unmanaged'/'no-match'. If that pure function is ever edited (e.g. a future arm64/musl fast-follow changes the decision table) so it returns 'proceed-unmanaged' or 'no-match' while selection.ok is true, this call site would silently fall through into walkBundleDir/encodeNodePtyPayload/runNodePtyInstall and attempt an install nobody asked for, rather than failing loudly -- no test exercises this fallthrough because the current pure implementation never produces it.
fix: Replace the comment-only narrowing with an exhaustive switch/if-chain over decision.kind (skip -> return undefined; install -> proceed; default -> throw an internal-invariant error naming the unexpected kind), so a future change to decideNodePtyProvision that violates the selection.ok invariant fails fast with a clear diagnostic instead of silently attempting an unintended install.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: The v1 prebuilt target set is hand-duplicated across four independent places with nothing but comments tying them together: PREBUILT_TARGETS_V1 in src/remote-client/prebuilt.ts, the separately-declared V1_TARGETS array in scripts/verify-node-pty-prebuild.mjs (a plain-Node/ESM script deliberately kept out of the TS tree), the if-condition inside selectPrebuiltTarget, and the literal string 'linux-x64-glibc only' hardcoded into two user-facing diagnostics in bootstrap.ts (noMatchDiagnostic and the proceed-unmanaged onDiagnostic message). Adding the documented arm64/musl fast-follow row requires updating all four by hand; missing one yields either a verify script that silently stops covering a real target, or stale diagnostic wording claiming a narrower target set than what the code actually supports.
fix: At minimum, add a cheap cross-check test that reads PREBUILT_TARGETS_V1 (or its source string) and asserts scripts/verify-node-pty-prebuild.mjs's V1_TARGETS contains the same entries, so the documented 'kept in lockstep by hand' comment is backed by a change-detector; consider deriving the diagnostic wording from PREBUILT_TARGETS_V1.join(...) instead of a hardcoded 'linux-x64-glibc only' string.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: The node-pty payload's integrity check covers only build/Release/pty.node's sha-256 (both the release-gate verify-node-pty-prebuild.mjs and the remote UNPACK_SRC unpacker). Every other shipped file -- package.json and the entire lib/**/*.js tree, all of which the agent process require()'s and executes on the remote host -- is only checked for byte-COUNT (short-read protection), never content integrity. A compromise of the release build machine/pipeline, or of the staged out/agent/prebuilds tree before packaging, that substitutes malicious bytes into any lib/*.js file (same length as the original) would ship inside every installer undetected by REQ-003's release gate and be silently require()'d as executable JavaScript on every remote host that gets (re)provisioned -- remote code execution with zero integrity signal, even though the sha-256 verification machinery already exists and is trivially extensible to the whole bundle.
fix: Extend the manifest (.termhalla-prebuilt.json) to carry a sha-256 (or single tree-hash) for every shipped file, not just pty.node; verify all of them in both verify-node-pty-prebuild.mjs (release gate) and UNPACK_SRC (remote unpacker) before promotion, exactly as pty.node is verified today.
resolution: Verified fixed in code (not just claimed): PrebuiltManifest.files now carries a sha-256 for EVERY shipped file (prebuilt.ts:50-59); scripts/stage-node-pty-prebuild.mjs's computeFilesMap hashes every staged file (excl. the marker); scripts/verify-node-pty-prebuild.mjs's verifyBundleFiles enforces bidirectional key-set parity + per-file sha at the release gate (TEST-2367 exercises the divergence case); UNPACK_SRC (prebuilt.ts:320-331) verifies EVERY received file's sha-256 in memory before any write, in addition to pty.node's dedicated check. Confirmed by running the real suite: node-pty-staging.test.ts, node-pty-probe-unpack.test.ts, node-pty-provision-flow.test.ts all green (72/72).

claim: release.yml's new build-node-pty-linux job installs Node inside the ubuntu:20.04 build container via 'curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_22.x | bash -' -- a curl-pipe-to-bash of a third-party script, fetched with no signature or checksum pinning, executed with root privileges in the same container that subsequently compiles the node-pty native binary shipped inside every released installer and pushed onto every remote host by this feature. A compromise of deb.nodesource.com, its CDN, or the network path (DNS hijack, TLS-terminating proxy on the runner) yields arbitrary code execution in the trusted release pipeline that produces a binary this feature auto-uploads to customer remote hosts with no further human review.
fix: Replace the curl|bash Node install with a pinned, checksum-verified artifact: use actions/setup-node (which verifies against Node's published checksums) or download the NodeSource .deb/tarball explicitly and verify its sha256 against a value recorded in the workflow before installing, rather than piping a fetched script directly into bash.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: release.yml's node-pty build/stage steps fetch the pinned node-pty version via 'npm pack node-pty@$VERSION' (twice: once in build-node-pty-linux, again in the release job's staging step) resolved only by version string, never checked against the sha512 'integrity' hash package-lock.json already records for that exact node-pty version (verified present at package-lock.json:5225). A registry-side substitution for that version, or a compromised registry mirror/proxy reachable from the GitHub-hosted runner, would let a tampered node-pty source ship as the release's native prebuilt without detection, since nothing in the release lane ties the fetched tarball back to the project's own vetted lockfile hash.
fix: After 'npm pack', compute the tarball's sha512 and compare it to the 'integrity' field recorded for node-pty in package-lock.json (parsed via node -p or a small script) before building from it; fail the job on mismatch.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: walkBundleDir (bootstrap.ts) and copyTree (stage-node-pty-prebuild.mjs) enumerate directory entries with UNSORTED readdirSync. In walkBundleDir this order fixes BOTH the NODE_PTY_PAYLOAD_V1 header entries AND the concatenated file-byte order of the upload payload, so the exact bytes streamed over the ssh install channel vary by filesystem/platform/run (readdir order is unspecified and OS-dependent). It is functionally harmless TODAY (header and bytes derive from the same walk so they stay self-consistent; UNPACK_SRC restores files by header path; the only integrity anchor is pty.node's sha-256; and every frozen test sorts its tree comparison, e.g. node-pty-probe-unpack.test.ts's tree() and the .sort()'d expected lists) — but it is a latent nondeterminism hazard: a future whole-payload hash, a reproducible-build assertion, an upload-dedup/cache keyed on payload bytes, or any test that pins the wire bytes would break intermittently. Same nondeterministic-ordering class in copyTree's staged-bundle copy order.
fix: Sort the readdir result before recursing/emitting in both spots, e.g. 'for (const name of readdirSync(d).sort())', so the streamed payload and the staged bundle are byte-order-reproducible across hosts and runs. propose CONV: when a filesystem directory listing (readdir/readdirSync) feeds a wire payload, a hash input, or a reproducible build artifact, sort the entries by name before use — never rely on the platform/filesystem enumeration order.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: REQ-021 glibc-floor hint is unreachable in the live connect flow. glibcFloorHint is only called inside asProvisionIneffectiveNodePty, which is reached only when looksLikeNodePtyResolutionFailure() returns true (it requires the diagnostic to match /cannot find module|module_not_found/). A genuine glibc-too-old dlopen failure produces stderr like 'version GLIBC_2.34 not found (required by .../node_modules/node-pty/build/Release/pty.node)', which matches the node-pty name pattern but NOT the module-resolution pattern, so the hint never fires for the exact failure class REQ-021 exists to cover (the one no-match case the probe cannot see). TEST-2333 only pins the pure glibcFloorHint function in isolation; no flow test exercises the wiring, so the dead path passed the gate.
fix: Call glibcFloorHint on any post-install agent-launch fatal (or whenever GLIBC_NOT_FOUND_RE matches the launch diagnostic), independent of the module-resolution detector. Add a flow-level test that rigs a launch stderr containing 'version GLIBC_2.34 not found' and asserts the returned fatal diagnostic contains 2.31 and the node_modules/node-pty escape hatch.
resolution: Verified fixed in code (bootstrap.ts + prebuilt.ts), not just claimed. glibcFloorHint (prebuilt.ts:598-601, regex GLIBC_[0-9]+(?:\.[0-9]+)*["']?\s*not found — matches a real dlopen 'version GLIBC_2.34 not found (required by .../node-pty/build/Release/pty.node)') is now applied in resolveLaunchFatal (bootstrap.ts:730-733) as the FIRST check, BEFORE and INDEPENDENT of looksLikeNodePtyResolutionFailure, for BOTH the first launch fatal (line 762) and the second launch fatal (line 784), and in runRecoveryCycle's relaunch (line 713). The glibc-first early-return also guarantees a GLIBC-class fatal NEVER triggers the recovery cycle (REQ-021). The dead-path (hint only reachable via the module-resolution detector) is gone. Flow-level coverage now exists: TEST-2376 (GLIBC-class launch fatal with NO module-resolution wording ⇒ diagnostic contains 2.31 + node_modules/node-pty escape hatch, ledger [probe, node-pty-install, launch] with NO recovery) PASSES, plus TEST-2333 unit. Ran both files 2026-07-06: green.

claim: The node-pty probe channel accumulates remote stdout with no cap (stdout += chunk.toString in runNodePtyProbe), whereas every sibling channel bounds its stderr tail to STDERR_TAIL_CHARS (400). A misconfigured or hostile remote whose non-interactive login shell streams unbounded stdout (a looping rc echo, or yes piped into the login banner) grows this string without limit inside the privileged Electron main process. Combined with the absence of any timeout on the probe, the connect never completes and memory trends toward exhaustion. REQ-009 anticipated rc noise around the sentinel but never bounded the total volume.
fix: Bound the accumulated probe stdout to a fixed window large enough to hold the sentinel line plus reasonable rc noise (e.g. retain only the last N KB) and stop growing past it. Optionally settle as soon as a sentinel line is decoded and tear the child down so an endless stdout stream cannot wedge the connect.
resolution: Verified fixed in code (bootstrap.ts runNodePtyProbe + prebuilt.ts), not just claimed. Probe stdout is no longer unbounded 'stdout += chunk': runNodePtyProbe (bootstrap.ts:473-478) now feeds every chunk through appendBoundedProbeStdout(stdoutWindow, chunk) bounded to the exported NODE_PTY_PROBE_STDOUT_CAP=65536 (prebuilt.ts:611-619, retains only the trailing cap chars, discards older on overflow), AND settles the MOMENT parseProbeStdout(stdoutWindow) !== null, calling settle→teardownChild (kill + destroy all three streams, the F19 posture) instead of waiting for stream end. So an endless-stdout remote can neither wedge the connect nor grow memory in the Electron main process. New REQ-026 codifies it. Coverage: TEST-2368 (cap exported == 65536; bounded window keeps ≤ cap trailing chars; a sentinel after >cap of noise still parses) and TEST-2377 (npty-probe-endless rig: sentinel then unbounded stdout ⇒ connect settles on sentinel and completes) both PASS. Ran 2026-07-06: green. (No internal wall-clock timeout was added — that liveness gap remains covered by the deferred FINDING-011, unchanged.)

claim: The new mandatory probe gate runs before every node-pty connect with no internal timeout and no ssh liveness options (buildSshExecArgv emits no ServerAliveInterval/ServerAliveCountMax/ConnectTimeout, and there is no setTimeout anywhere in src/remote-client). A stalled remote node, or a half-open TCP connection (network dropped after auth), leaves runNodePtyProbe pending forever; the connect hangs indefinitely and only an external AbortSignal recovers. This hang surface pre-dates the feature (F19 connect/provision share it), but the probe adds a serial precondition to every connect that amplifies it. REQ-016/REQ-017 designed cancellation solely around AbortSignal, so a caller with no wired cancel has no recovery.
fix: Give the probe and install channels a bounded liveness guard: either an internal timeout that settles a specific actionable fatal, or pass ssh -o ServerAliveInterval and -o ServerAliveCountMax (and/or -o ConnectTimeout) in the exec argv so a dead connection self-terminates while still permitting interactive 2FA/hardware-key auth (do not add BatchMode).
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: The client uploads the actual local build/Release/pty.node bytes but stamps the NODE_PTY_PAYLOAD_V1 header with the manifest-claimed ptyNodeSha256 (encodeNodePtyPayload(files, local.manifest.ptyNodeSha256)) without ever verifying the two agree. readLocalManifest validates structure and existence only and never re-hashes the local pty.node. So a locally-corrupt or partially-downloaded bundle (manifest sha does not match its own pty.node) fails on the REMOTE as sentinel 94 with the diagnostic 'a sha-256 checksum mismatch was detected verifying the received pty.node - retry the connect', mislabeling a purely local fault as a transfer/remote failure and advising an infinite retry that can never repair a local condition. REQ-019 only enumerates missing-file/malformed-manifest as local failure modes, so this gap slipped through.
fix: Recompute sha-256 of the local build/Release/pty.node in readLocalManifest and compare to the manifest ptyNodeSha256; on mismatch fail before any upload with a specific LOCAL-bundle-corrupt fatal naming the local path and advising re-staging/re-downloading the installer, not 'retry the connect'.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: REQ-015 lists as a MUST-hold consequence that concurrent node-pty installs are safe (last promote wins, both complete). But UNPACK_SRC promotes via fs.rmSync(finalDir) then fs.renameSync(tmp, finalDir). Two connects racing the same host interleave as A.rm, B.rm, A.rename, B.rename; B.rename onto the now-populated finalDir throws ENOTEMPTY on Linux / EPERM on Windows (empirically confirmed here), so the loser does NOT complete -- its promote catch runs fail(BYTES_EXIT), which the client reports as a misleading byte-count/short-read mismatch (a CONV-001 mis-attribution -- it was a promote collision, not a byte mismatch). last promote wins is also false (first-to-populate wins). No test exercises concurrency, so the MUST is both unmet and unverified.
fix: Make the final rename race-tolerant AND add a concurrency acceptance vector. e.g. on ENOTEMPTY/EEXIST at the final rename, treat it as a benign lost race (another connect already promoted an identical sha-verified install), verify the now-present marker equals the shipped manifest, and exit 0; or serialize via a remote lock / atomic renameat2 RENAME_EXCHANGE. Add an unpacker-under-local-node test running two installs with overlapping promote windows and assert BOTH exit 0 with the final dir intact. propose CONV: a spec consequence written as MUST-hold (a concurrency/idempotency/atomicity guarantee) MUST have its own acceptance vector at the tests phase, never only prose.
resolution: Verified fixed in code, not just claimed. src/remote-client/prebuilt.ts UNPACK_SRC promote (lines 306-349) now catches the rename collision (errCode ENOTEMPTY/EEXIST/EPERM), reads the now-present final-dir marker, compares its ptyNodeSha256 to the payload header sha, and exits 0 on an identical (benign lost race) install or NODE_PTY_RACE_EXIT=95 on a divergent/absent-marker install — a collision is NEVER reported as 93. bootstrap.ts runNodePtyInstall (lines 543-547) maps 95 to a distinct fatal with concurrent-install-collision + reconnect wording (never truncat/checksum). The mandated concurrency acceptance vectors now exist and drive the REAL unpacker through a genuine fs-rename collision via tests/fixtures/npty-race-interpose.cjs: TEST-2360 (two identical installs → BOTH exit 0, final dir intact, no *.tmp), TEST-2362 (divergent → 95, both shas named), TEST-2363 (no-marker → 95). REQ-015 amended and re-propagated. The original defect (collision misreported as byte/short-read 93; concurrency MUST unmet+unverified) is closed.

claim: The co-provision design opens a separate ssh exec channel (the read-only probe) before EVERY connect, including steady-state reconnects -- the REQ-016 acceptance (TEST-2344) pins the second-connect ledger as [probe, launch]. Each exec channel is an independent system-ssh invocation with no ControlMaster/multiplexing (buildSshExecArgv emits no -o ControlPath), so it re-runs authentication. For a host with 2FA / hardware-key / password auth, the probe adds one extra interactive auth challenge to EVERY reconnect forever (steady-state channels 1 -> 2; fresh-host 3 -> 5). The intake/spec acknowledge per-channel prompts inherited from F19 but neither count the new per-connect total nor mitigate the amplification the always-on probe introduces.
fix: Amortize the added auth cost. Options: (a) multiplex a connects exec channels over a single ssh ControlMaster/ControlPath session so probe+install+launch+upload authenticate once; (b) fold the probe into the launch, or cache a recent skip decision keyed by host+agentDir+version, so a steady-state reconnect opens no extra channel; or (c) at minimum, state the new per-connect channel/prompt count in REQ-016 as an explicitly accepted tradeoff for interactive-auth users.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: PROBE_SRC derives glibc SOLELY from process.report.getReport().header.glibcVersionRuntime, defaulting to null whenever process.report is unavailable or getReport is not a function. deriveLibc then returns non-glibc, so a genuine linux-x64-GLIBC host whose node cannot self-report glibc (an older or restricted node -- precisely the locked-down/offline enterprise hosts this feature exists to serve) is classified linux/x64/non-glibc and rejected as no-match FATAL, with a diagnostic naming libc=non-glibc. REQ-010 lumps musl and unknown together, but a report-incapable glibc host is a THIRD case the no-match wording misreports -- and the escape hatch it suggests (manually install node-pty) would in fact have succeeded on that glibc host. Detection hinges on a runtime self-report the target demographic may not provide, and nothing tests the report-absent-but-glibc path.
fix: Add a fallback glibc signal for when process.report is absent (e.g. the probe additionally checks for a glibc marker such as /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6, or parses ldd --version), and/or make the no-match diagnostic distinguish confirmed-musl from unknown/undetectable-libc so the operator learns the real cause instead of a possibly-false non-glibc. At minimum, name process.report unavailability as a candidate cause in the no-match wording so a genuine glibc host is not turned away by a misleading message.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: prebuilt.ts and bootstrap.ts each independently define their own sanitizeStderr control-character stripper (same character class: 0x00-0x1f, 0x7f-0x9f; built two different ways, a literal regex in bootstrap.ts vs a codes-array-built RegExp in prebuilt.ts) instead of sharing one. Worse than mere duplication: runNodePtyProbe/runNodePtyInstall in bootstrap.ts sanitize each stderr chunk into stderrTail with bootstrap.ts's own sanitizeStderr, then pass that already-clean text as stderrExcerpt into classifyProbeOutcome in prebuilt.ts, whose exit-255/no-sentinel diagnostics call prebuilt.ts's OWN separate sanitizeStderr on it again: redundant double-sanitization across the module boundary, and a second, distinct instance (beyond FINDING-001's SAFE_REMOTE_PATH/SAFE_NONCE duplication) of this feature reimplementing a sibling helper instead of importing it.
fix: Export one sanitizeStderr from a shared location (ssh-command.ts or a small shared text-sanitization module) and have both bootstrap.ts and prebuilt.ts import it, so stderr is sanitized exactly once per byte and the two copies cannot silently diverge.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: The client's skip-vs-install decision (decideNodePtyProvision in prebuilt.ts, fed by PROBE_SRC in the same file) trusts the remote marker file's self-reported ptyNodeSha256 field verbatim -- it never re-hashes the actual build/Release/pty.node bytes currently on disk at probe time. PROBE_SRC only JSON.parse's the marker sidecar file; it never reads or hashes pty.node itself. Sha-256 verification (both UNPACK_SRC's remote check and the release gate) runs exactly ONCE, at install/promote time; every subsequent connect's skip decision is a cache hit against that one-time-verified marker's claimed value, not a re-verification of what is actually present. So once node-pty is installed, anyone/anything with independent filesystem write access to <agentDir>/node_modules/node-pty on the remote host (a compromised sibling process, another local user, a tampering supply-chain step) can overwrite build/Release/pty.node with an arbitrary native module while leaving the marker JSON's ptyNodeSha256 field untouched (or copied verbatim) -- every future connect from the trusted Termhalla client will see the marker fields match, resolves===true, decide 'skip', and never detect the substitution. The agent then require()s the tampered native code every launch. This defeats the entire purpose of the sha-256 verification machinery this feature otherwise takes great care with (including a race-tolerant promote that itself trusts the same marker-vs-marker comparison, FINDING-013): the file whose integrity is verified and the file whose claimed-integrity is trusted on every subsequent connect are never the same read.
fix: Have the probe (PROBE_SRC) also read + sha-256 hash the actual build/Release/pty.node bytes at <agentDir>/node_modules/node-pty/build/Release/pty.node and report that ACTUAL hash as a new probe field (e.g. actualPtyNodeSha256), separate from the marker's claimed value. decideNodePtyProvision's skip condition should require actualPtyNodeSha256 === local.manifest.ptyNodeSha256 (not just marker.ptyNodeSha256 === local.manifest.ptyNodeSha256), so a marker/binary mismatch on the remote forces a reinstall instead of silently skipping forever.
resolution: Verified fixed in code (not just claimed): PROBE_SRC (prebuilt.ts:187-193) now re-reads and sha-256-hashes the ACTUAL on-disk build/Release/pty.node bytes into actualPtyNodeSha256, separate from the marker's self-claimed ptyNodeSha256. decideNodePtyProvision's skip branch (prebuilt.ts:552-559) requires probe.actualPtyNodeSha256 === localManifest.ptyNodeSha256 in addition to the marker-field match, so a marker/binary divergence now forces install (self-repair) instead of a silent forever-skip. The promote-collision benign-lost-race check in UNPACK_SRC also independently re-hashes the present install's actual binary (readActualPtyNodeSha, prebuilt.ts:388-390) rather than trusting the marker alone. Confirmed via the real test suite: TEST-2373 (self-repair over corrupted on-disk pty.node under an intact matching marker -> install, not skip) passes; full node-pty-*.test.ts suite green (72/72).

location: src/remote-client/prebuilt.ts:300-304,346-348 (UNPACK_SRC staging/promote catches); bootstrap.ts:533-537 (93 client wording) / REQ-015
claim: UNPACK_SRC folds every NON-collision remote filesystem failure during staging and promote into NODE_PTY_BYTES_EXIT (93). The staging-write catch (prebuilt.ts:300-304, "failed writing the staged node-pty install") and the non-collision promote catch (prebuilt.ts:346-348, "failed to promote the staged node-pty install") both call fail(BYTES_EXIT). bootstrap.ts:533-537 maps 93 to "a byte-count/short-read mismatch was detected while streaming the prebuilt payload -- nothing was promoted remotely; retry the connect". So a PERSISTENT remote-side fs condition -- ENOSPC (disk full), EACCES/EPERM (permission), EROFS (read-only home) -- is mis-attributed as a transient transfer/byte-count mismatch and the operator is told to "retry the connect", which can never repair a full disk or an unwritable home. This is the SAME mis-classification class the FINDING-013/ESC-001 amendment fixed for the ENOTEMPTY collision case (a collision is never a byte-count failure), but it persists for the remaining fs-error classes: REQ-015's sentinel taxonomy (93 byte-count / 94 sha / 95 race) has no code or wording for a remote filesystem write/promote error. The raw e.message (e.g. "ENOSPC: no space left on device") IS appended as the stderr tail (mitigation), but the primary diagnostic and the retry advice remain wrong-actionable, and no test exercises a staging/promote fs error.
fix: Give remote-side filesystem write/promote failures (ENOSPC/EACCES/EPERM/EROFS) a distinct sentinel (or a generic non-transient "remote install could not be written" code) so the client diagnostic names a persistent remote condition and does NOT advise "retry the connect"; at minimum, change the 93 client wording so it does not promise a retry will help when the stderr tail carries a non-transient fs error code. Add an unpacker-under-local-node test that rigs a promote/staging fs error and asserts the diagnostic is not the transfer/byte-count "retry" wording. propose CONV: a remote-side failure sentinel must map to client advice matching the failure's persistence -- never advise "retry" for a non-transient remote condition (disk full / permission / read-only).
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

location: src/remote-client/prebuilt.ts:306-349 (UNPACK_SRC amended promote / collision handler) / REQ-015
claim: The amended race-tolerant promote resolves a rename collision by reading the now-present finalDir marker EXACTLY ONCE (prebuilt.ts:321-329) and treating an absent/unparseable marker as a DIVERGENT install => exit 95. That marker re-read is itself an unguarded TOCTOU: between the failed rename and the readFileSync, a THIRD concurrent connect (or the winner's own next reinstall) can be mid-fs.rmSync(finalDir,{recursive}) or between its own rm and rename, so the loser reads a missing/partial marker (observedSha=null) and reports exit 95 for what is actually an IDENTICAL install -- failing a connect that REQ-015's idempotency guarantee ("two racing connects shipping the same release BOTH complete") intends to succeed. The mandated concurrency acceptance vectors (TEST-2360/2362/2363) interpose exactly ONE other promoted install and never exercise a third promoter rm-ing finalDir during the marker read, so the window passed the gate untested. Impact is bounded: the connect self-heals on the next reconnect (the probe then sees the identical install and decides skip), so the effect is a spurious one-shot connect failure under 3+ simultaneous connects to the same host+agentDir -- but exit 95's diagnostic ("another connect got there first with a divergent payload") is then simply false.
fix: Make the collision resolution robust to a racing re-remove: retry the marker read a bounded number of times, and/or when the marker is missing (finalDir absent => no winner currently present) attempt one more rename of the temp into place before concluding "divergent" -- only conclude exit 95 when a stable, readable marker with a genuinely different ptyNodeSha256 is observed. Add a 3-way concurrency vector (a third promoter rm-ing finalDir during the loser's marker read) asserting identical installs never spuriously exit 95.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-05 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW quality/cleanup debt, out of scope for this feature's fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: The skip decision is blind to a torn REMOTE native binary, giving a permanent unrecoverable connect wedge. decideNodePtyProvision computes manifestsMatch from the remote marker file ptyNodeSha256 — the CLAIMED value written at install time, NEVER recomputed from the on-disk bytes — and PROBE_SRC resolves is only require.resolve of node-pty (true from package.json + lib/index.js; it never dlopens build/Release/pty.node). So if a previously-installed remote node-pty keeps its JS + marker but its build/Release/pty.node is later truncated / corrupted / deleted (disk-full, partial user copy, AV quarantine), the probe reports marker-match AND resolves===true, decideNodePtyProvision returns skip, ZERO upload happens, and the agent launch dies on require(../build/Release/pty.node). looksLikeNodePtyResolutionFailure then returns asProvisionIneffectiveNodePty whose text claims 'exactly one node-pty install was applied and no further attempts will be made' — BOTH false in the skip path (no install ran this connect) — and every reconnect re-skips and re-fails identically: a permanent wedge with no self-repair and a misleading terminal diagnostic. This defeats REQ-012 stated intent (a torn/hand-damaged install is repaired, never trusted) and the REQ-015 invariant that a marker at the final path implies a complete sha-verified install (true only at write time, never re-verified at skip). No test exercises marker-match + resolves-true + corrupt-on-disk-pty.node.
fix: Make skip verify the on-disk native binary, not just JS resolution + the self-claimed marker sha. Have PROBE_SRC recompute sha-256 of build/Release/pty.node under node_modules/node-pty (or return a nativeLoads boolean from an actual process.dlopen of the addon in a child) and gate skip on that recomputed value equalling the local manifest sha, so a corrupt native binary forces install (self-repair) instead of a permanent fatal. At minimum, do not emit 'exactly one install was applied' wording on a skip path where no install ran, and advise deleting <agentDir>/node_modules/node-pty to recover. Add a flow test: marker-match + resolves-true + corrupted remote pty.node must reinstall (not skip) and the connect must succeed. propose CONV: an idempotency/skip decision that trusts a self-written integrity marker MUST re-verify the artifact it guards from ground truth (re-hash/reload), never trust the marker's own claimed hash.
resolution: Verified fixed in code, not merely claimed. PROBE_SRC (src/remote-client/prebuilt.ts:187-193) now reads the ACTUAL bytes at agentDir/node_modules/node-pty/build/Release/pty.node via node:crypto and reports actualPtyNodeSha256 (null when absent/unreadable), independent of the marker claim. decideNodePtyProvision (prebuilt.ts:552-557) gates skip on probe.actualPtyNodeSha256 === localManifest.ptyNodeSha256 in addition to marker equality and resolves===true, so a torn/corrupted/deleted on-disk pty.node under an intact marker returns install (self-repair). The dishonest terminal wording is gone: terminalNodePtyFatal (bootstrap.ts:690-696) now emits a distinct skip-path message ('a previously installed node-pty was found and verified on disk ... yet the agent still could not load it') that no longer claims an install was applied, and names the remove-and-reconnect escape hatch. The self-repair flow vector exists (TEST-2373: intact matching marker + resolvable JS + corrupted on-disk pty.node via npty-preseed-corrupt rig => decision install, binary repaired, hello reached), plus TEST-2330/2369/2370 (probe ground-truth) and TEST-2375 (skip-honesty wording). The original permanent-wedge-on-corrupt-binary defect for pty.node is closed.

claim: The ESC-001/ESC-002 fix made the UNPACKER promote-collision race-safe, but the CONNECT FLOW under concurrent same-host connects is still not race-safe, and no vector tests install-then-launch under a racing installer (the concurrency vectors run the unpacker in ISOLATION, asserting both processes exit 0 — never the full connectWithProvisioning both reach hello). UNPACK_SRC promotes via non-atomic fs.rmSync(finalDir) then fs.renameSync(tmp, finalDir). Two connects X,Y to the same agentDir: X installs (exit 0), coProvisionNodePty returns undefined, and connectWithProvisioning proceeds to connectAgent which launches the agent and does import(node-pty) resolving from finalDir. If Y is concurrently between its own rm(finalDir) (which just removed X's freshly-promoted install) and its rename, X's agent import observes finalDir ABSENT → ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND → agent dies before hello → looksLikeNodePtyResolutionFailure → asProvisionIneffectiveNodePty: a spurious hard fatal saying 'exactly one install was applied and no further attempts will be made', even though a plain reconnect would skip-succeed. REQ-015 reasoned only about the promote collision (both unpackers exit 0); REQ-020 tested the launch single-connect only; the rm-then-rename absent-window intersecting a concurrent agent launch is an ignored case — 'both racing connects complete successfully' is met at the unpacker level but not at the flow level.
fix: Close the absent-window between install success and the agent module resolution. Either make the promote atomic from a reader viewpoint (rename onto finalDir without a prior rm — on the ENOTEMPTY collision run the REQ-015 sha-compare/replace path — so finalDir is never transiently absent for an identical/newer install), OR when a post-co-provision agent launch fails with a node-pty resolution error and this connect ran/skipped a node-pty install, re-probe once and retry the launch instead of emitting a terminal 'no further attempts' fatal. Add a flow-level concurrency vector: two overlapping connectWithProvisioning calls against the same fake home both reach hello (not merely both unpackers exit 0).
resolution: Verified fixed in code. UNPACK_SRC (src/remote-client/prebuilt.ts:369-427) now promotes RENAME-FIRST with no preceding rm of the final dir: rename success exits 0 (final dir never absent); on a collision it reads the present marker AND the on-disk binary and, when both equal ours, treats it as a benign lost race that exits 0 WITHOUT removing the final dir (the identical-install loser never creates an absent window); only a divergent/absent install triggers the sanctioned rm+retry, with a persistent divergence exiting 95. The connect-FLOW recovery exists: resolveLaunchFatal/runRecoveryCycle (bootstrap.ts:698-739) run exactly ONE re-probe -> re-decide -> (<=1 install) -> relaunch when co-provisioning engaged and a launch dies with a node-pty module-resolution error, capped at two probes/installs/launches. The mandated flow-level concurrency vector exists (TEST-2379: two overlapping connectWithProvisioning against the same fresh home => BOTH reach hello, exactly one final install, no *.tmp) alongside the unpacker-level reader-atomicity pins (TEST-2360/2372 via the npty-race-interpose op log proving no blind pre-rename rm). The original rm-then-rename absent-window intersecting a concurrent agent launch is closed for the same-release case.

claim: readLocalManifest (bootstrap.ts:394-403) validates the manifest's 'files' field only as 'typeof === object' (an array passes too; per-key values are never checked to be strings), and the files-building step in runCoProvisionPass (bootstrap.ts:647-653) silently falls back to self-computing a file's sha256 FROM ITS OWN LOCAL BYTES whenever local.manifest.files[f.path] is missing/undefined -- 'local.manifest.files[f.path] ?? createHash(sha256).update(f.bytes).digest(hex)'. This defeats the entire per-file integrity property FINDING-005/REQ-014 exists to provide: REQ-014 states each payload entry's sha256 MUST be 'sourced from the local manifest's files map', but a manifest with an incomplete, empty ({}), or array-shaped files map produces a payload where every uncovered file's header sha256 is trivially self-consistent (computed from the exact bytes being hashed), so the remote unpacker's per-file verification in UNPACK_SRC can never catch a corrupt/tampered local file in that case -- it always matches. Contrast with scripts/verify-node-pty-prebuild.mjs's verifyBundleFiles, which enforces full key-set parity in BOTH directions (uncovered staged file -> fail; ghost map key -> fail) at the release gate -- the client-side reader of the exact same manifest shape (readLocalManifest) enforces none of that. No test exercises this: both tests/node-pty-provision-flow.test.ts:111 and tests/integration-node-pty-prebuilt.test.ts:109 always build a files map covering every staged file, so the fallback branch has zero coverage.
fix: In readLocalManifest, validate 'files' is a plain (non-array) object whose values are all non-empty strings, AND validate its key set covers at least every file the bundle directory actually contains (mirroring verifyBundleFiles's staged-vs-mapped parity check) before returning ok:true -- fail with a specific 'local manifest incomplete/malformed' diagnostic naming the missing/extra path(s), the same class of fatal REQ-019 already mandates for a malformed manifest. Then delete the '?? createHash(...)' fallback in runCoProvisionPass entirely -- once readLocalManifest guarantees completeness, a missing files[f.path] can only mean an internal invariant violation and should throw, never silently self-hash. Add a unit test that stages a files map missing one lib/*.js entry and asserts the connect fails fatal before any upload, rather than shipping a self-consistent hash for that file.
resolution: Verified fixed: bootstrap.ts readLocalManifest now calls validateManifestFiles which rejects non-object/array files fields, validates every value is a non-empty string, and enforces bidirectional path-set parity against walkBundleDirPaths(bundleDir) before returning ok:true. The '?? createHash(...)' self-compute fallback in runCoProvisionPass's file-building step is gone -- sha256 is now local.manifest.files[f.path] unconditionally (marker file excepted, computed fresh by design). Covered by TEST-2380/2381/2382 in tests/node-pty-provision-flow.test.ts (removed-from-map, ghost-key, malformed-shape vectors, all asserting pre-upload fatal).

claim: coProvisionNodePty's client-side payload builder (bootstrap.ts:647-653) enumerates the LOCAL bundle dir dynamically via walkBundleDir (a plain recursive readdir, by design per REQ-022/TEST-2004) and computes each file's upload sha256 as (local.manifest.files[f.path] ?? createHash('sha256').update(f.bytes).digest('hex')) -- i.e. for any file present on disk under <prebuiltRoot>/node-pty/<target>/ that is NOT a key in the manifest's files map, the client falls back to hashing the file's own (possibly tampered/injected) bytes and ships that self-computed hash as the header sha256. Because both UNPACK_SRC's per-file check (gotSha computed from received bytes vs entrySha) and the whole point of FINDING-005's fix is verifying uploaded bytes against a value the client did NOT just derive from those same bytes, this fallback is a no-op integrity check for exactly the files the manifest does not cover -- any locally added/rogue file in the staged bundle dir (e.g. dropped there by a compromised sibling process, a buggy plugin, or a corrupted local install of the shipped resources) is uploaded to <agentDir>/node_modules/node-pty/ on the remote host with a self-consistent-by-construction hash that always 'verifies', silently defeating the manifest-based whole-payload integrity goal for anything outside the originally-staged file set. The release-time gate (scripts/verify-node-pty-prebuild.mjs's verifyBundleFiles) DOES enforce bidirectional file-set parity against the manifest, but that check runs only once at release-build time -- it is never re-run against the LOCAL bundle dir at connect time on the end user's already-installed app, so a bundle dir that drifts from its manifest after installation (locally, on the operator's own machine) gets no client-side detection at all, and an attacker-planted extra file lands on the remote host unchecked.
fix: In coProvisionNodePty (bootstrap.ts), reject (fatal, before any upload) rather than self-hash-and-accept any file discovered by walkBundleDir whose relative path is not a key in local.manifest.files (other than the marker file itself) -- mirror the release gate's bidirectional file-set parity check (verifyBundleFiles) at connect time against the LOCAL bundle dir, so a locally-tampered/drifted staged bundle is caught before ssh, not silently uploaded with a fabricated true-by-construction hash.
resolution: Superseded by FINDING-024: same defect, refiled with --contract-violation set because it violates REQ-014's MUST ('each entry's sha256 sourced from the local manifest's files map') — the CLI has no edit-finding command so the flag could not be added in place.

claim: coProvisionNodePty's client-side payload builder (bootstrap.ts:647-653) enumerates the LOCAL bundle dir dynamically via walkBundleDir and computes each file's upload sha256 as (local.manifest.files[f.path] ?? createHash('sha256').update(f.bytes).digest('hex')) -- i.e. for any file present on disk under <prebuiltRoot>/node-pty/<target>/ that is NOT a key in the manifest's files map, the client falls back to hashing the file's own (possibly tampered/injected) bytes and ships that self-computed hash as the header sha256, rather than the manifest-sourced value REQ-014 mandates ('each entry's sha256 sourced from the local manifest's files map' -- a MUST). Because UNPACK_SRC's per-file check compares received-bytes-hash against header-claimed-hash, and the header-claimed-hash for an unlisted file is derived from those SAME bytes, this is a no-op integrity check for exactly the files the manifest does not cover: any locally added/rogue file in the staged bundle dir (dropped there by a compromised sibling process, a buggy plugin, or local drift/corruption of the shipped resources after install) is uploaded to <agentDir>/node_modules/node-pty/ on the remote host with a self-consistent-by-construction hash that always 'verifies'. The release-time gate (scripts/verify-node-pty-prebuild.mjs's verifyBundleFiles) DOES enforce bidirectional file-set parity against the manifest, but only once at release-build time -- never re-run against the LOCAL bundle dir at connect time on the end user's already-installed app, so an attacker-planted or drifted extra file lands on the remote host with zero client-side detection.
fix: In coProvisionNodePty (bootstrap.ts), reject (fatal, before any upload) any file discovered by walkBundleDir whose relative path is not a key in local.manifest.files (other than the marker file, whose hash is legitimately computed fresh) -- mirror the release gate's bidirectional file-set parity check (verifyBundleFiles) at connect time against the LOCAL bundle dir, so a locally-tampered/drifted staged bundle is caught before ssh, and every uploaded sha256 is genuinely manifest-sourced as REQ-014 requires.
resolution: Verified fixed in code: bootstrap.ts's validateManifestFiles (lines 395-428) enforces bidirectional parity between the manifest files map and the on-disk bundle dir (uncoveredOnDisk + ghostInMap checks), called from readLocalManifest BEFORE any decision/upload; the payload builder (lines 712-718) no longer has a '?? createHash(...).update(f.bytes)' self-compute fallback for non-marker files -- sha256 is sourced exclusively from local.manifest.files[f.path], which is now guaranteed present by the parity check. Ran tests/node-pty-provision-flow.test.ts: 27/27 pass, including TEST-2380 (tampered+unmapped file rejected pre-upload), TEST-2381 (ghost manifest key rejected), TEST-2382 (malformed files shape: array / empty-string sha rejected).

claim: TEST-2351 aborts the in-flight probe with a bare wall-clock timer, setTimeout(() => ctl.abort(), 300), then asserts kinds() === [probe]. This races the fake-ssh child: the probe ledger line is appended by fake-ssh.mjs at its own startup (line 140), and the same file documents this box measures the probe leg at ~250 ms (dominated by node child-spawn latency), leaving only a ~50 ms margin before the 300 ms abort. If spawn latency exceeds ~300 ms on a loaded/slow machine, the abort kills the fake-ssh child before it logs, so kinds() === [] and the toEqual([probe]) assertion fails nondeterministically. The tests phase itself flagged THIS exact 300 ms timer as one that provably races this box 250 ms probe leg and hardened the sibling TEST-2352 to synchronize on the observed ledger via waitUntil(); TEST-2351 was left on the bare timer even though the deterministic waitUntil helper already lives in the same file (line 154).
fix: Mirror the TEST-2352 hardening: replace the wall-clock timer with await waitUntil(() => kinds().includes(probe), the probe channel to open) then ctl.abort(). Because the stall rig holds the probe channel open forever after it logs probe, aborting after the ledger line deterministically hits the in-flight probe and yields kinds() === [probe] on any machine. propose CONV: a test that must act mid-async-operation (abort/kill/mutate while a channel is open) MUST synchronize on an observed signal (a ledger line, a state file, or an awaited event), never a fixed wall-clock timer racing process spawn or channel setup.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness refinement, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: The load-bearing core assumption (intake decision 2, concept D2) is that one prebuilt per (os,arch,libc) is portable across ALL Node versions because node-pty is N-API (node-addon-api ^7.1.1, verified in node_modules). But N-API portability has a FLOOR: a pty.node compiled against node-addon-api 7 requires the remote node to support that N-API version (roughly Node >= 12.22 / 14.17). A remote below that floor — exactly the locked-down/offline/old enterprise hosts this feature targets — passes the probe (linux,x64,glibc all detected), gets an install, promotes and sha-verifies fine, then the agent dlopen of pty.node fails with an opaque N-API/ABI error like 'The module was compiled against a different Node.js version' / 'Node-API version X not supported'. That stderr matches NEITHER GLIBC_NOT_FOUND_RE (so glibcFloorHint stays empty) NOR MODULE_RESOLUTION_FAILURE_RE (so no recovery cycle), so resolveLaunchFatal passes the raw stderr through with zero actionable guidance and no floor hint. The glibc-too-old floor got an entire MUST (REQ-021) with a dedicated hint; the analogous Node/N-API-too-old floor — the SAME 'host is older than the shipped binary supports' class — got nothing, even though PROBE_SRC already collects node:process.version (line 201) which is then dead data, read nowhere in the flow. The spec never states a minimum remote Node version and no test exercises an N-API-incompatible host.
fix: Add a Node/N-API floor analogous to REQ-021's glibc floor: either (a) have decideNodePtyProvision/classify compare the probe's node:process.version against the minimum Node the shipped prebuilt requires and no-match FATAL early with an actionable diagnostic (naming the detected node version, the required minimum, and the escape hatch), or (b) detect the N-API/ABI-class dlopen stderr in resolveLaunchFatal and append a floor hint like the glibc one, and never enter the recovery cycle for it (reinstalling the same binary cannot help an old runtime). Document the minimum supported remote Node version in the spec/feature doc. propose CONV: when shipping a prebuilt native binary a remote will dlopen, the client MUST diagnose every too-old-runtime floor it can hit (glibc AND Node/N-API ABI) with a specific actionable message, never pass an opaque loader error straight through.
location: src/remote-client/prebuilt.ts:201 (PROBE_SRC emits node:process.version, never consumed); decideNodePtyProvision / REQ-008, REQ-021
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness refinement, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: The ESC-003/FINDING-020 ground-truth skip fix is incomplete: it re-verifies only build/Release/pty.node from disk (probe.actualPtyNodeSha256), NOT the lib/**/*.js files the agent also require()s at launch — even though the marker's files map already carries a sha-256 for every one of them. The skip gate (prebuilt.ts:552-557) checks marker equality + resolves===true + actualPtyNodeSha256 match; resolves is only require.resolve of node-pty, which verifies lib/index.js EXISTS but never that lib/unixTerminal.js / lib/terminal.js / lib/utils.js / lib/eventEmitter2.js exist or are intact. So a lib file that is truncated, corrupted, or deleted while the marker and pty.node stay intact yields the SAME permanent no-self-repair wedge FINDING-020 fixed for the native binary: probe reports marker-match + resolves-true + actualPtyNodeSha256-match => skip => launch dies requiring the torn lib file. Worse, the recovery cycle does NOT self-repair it: the re-probe re-skips (pty.node still matches) so the relaunch fails identically, and if the torn file is a SyntaxError rather than a missing-module it does not even match MODULE_RESOLUTION_FAILURE_RE so no recovery runs at all. The ground-truth gate was generalized to only one of the many files whose integrity a successful launch depends on, leaving the FINDING-020 wedge class open for the entire JS payload. No test exercises marker-match + resolves-true + intact-pty.node + torn-lib-file.
fix: Extend the ground-truth skip gate to the whole payload, not just pty.node: have PROBE_SRC hash every file named in the marker's files map (or a single tree-hash over them) and report it, and gate skip on all of them matching the local manifest — the marker already contains the per-file shas needed. A torn lib file then forces reinstall (self-repair) exactly as a torn pty.node now does. propose CONV: a ground-truth re-verification that guards a skip/idempotency decision MUST cover EVERY file the guarded operation depends on, not a single representative file — a partial re-verify leaves the same wedge open for the un-checked files.
location: src/remote-client/prebuilt.ts:187-193 (PROBE_SRC hashes only pty.node), :552-557 (decideNodePtyProvision skip gate) / REQ-012
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness refinement, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: On a MATCHED target (linux-x64-glibc), runCoProvisionPass reads the LOCAL bundle manifest and hard-FATALs when it is missing (bootstrap.ts:635-637, REQ-019) BEFORE ever calling decideNodePtyProvision — so the fatal fires even when the remote already has a fully resolvable node-pty (probe.resolves===true). The unmatched-target path honors that same escape hatch by returning proceed-unmanaged when the remote resolves, but the matched-target path denies it: a working remote is made unreachable purely because THIS client lacks a local bundle. This bites the dev workflow specifically — REQ-019 itself notes a dev checkout lacks a Linux prebuilt by design, and services.ts wires prebuiltRoot=out/agent/prebuilds which is empty until the staging script runs. So a developer on npm run dev connecting (default ptyBackend node-pty) to any linux-x64-glibc remote that ALREADY has node-pty installed (e.g. a host a released build provisioned earlier, like OpsHub) gets a hard fatal telling them to run the staging script / that the installer ships a bundle — advice that is beside the point because no install is needed at all; the remote resolves. This is a regression versus pre-feature behavior (before 0023 a dev could connect to a remote that already had node-pty) and an inconsistency: proceed-unmanaged is offered on unmatched-but-resolvable but withheld on matched-but-resolvable-and-no-local-bundle. The spec (REQ-019) mandates the fatal without considering the probe.resolves===true interaction, so no test covers matched target + local bundle absent + remote already resolves.
fix: In runCoProvisionPass, when the target matches but the local bundle is missing/invalid, first check probe.resolves: if the remote already resolves node-pty, take the proceed-unmanaged path (emit one diagnostic that an existing remote node-pty is being trusted because no local bundle is available to compare/replace it) rather than an unconditional fatal; only fatal (REQ-019) when the remote does NOT resolve and thus genuinely needs an install this client cannot supply. Add a test: matched target + empty prebuiltRoot + remote resolves => connect proceeds (proceed-unmanaged), no upload, no fatal.
location: src/remote-client/bootstrap.ts:635-637 (readLocalManifest fatal before decideNodePtyProvision, ignores probe.resolves) / REQ-019
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness refinement, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: The ESC-003 honest-terminal-wording mandate (REQ-016: the terminal diagnostic must state what this connect ACTUALLY did) is violated on the F19 no-recovery path. terminalNodePtyFatal(installed=true) always emits 'a node-pty install was applied (AND RE-VERIFIED ON A SECOND PROBE)' (bootstrap.ts:693). That phrase is honest only when reached via runRecoveryCycle, which runs an actual second probe. But terminalNodePtyFatal is ALSO reached from resolveLaunchFatal with canRecover=false (bootstrap.ts:736) — the F19 path: co-provision installs node-pty (installedEver=true), the first agent launch is 'absent' (normal on a fresh host — no agent .cjs yet), provisionAgent uploads the agent, then the SECOND launch dies with a node-pty module-resolution error. In that path only ONE node-pty probe ever ran (the co-provision pass) — the recovery cycle was NOT invoked (canRecover is false because the two-launch cap is spent) — yet the terminal diagnostic asserts the install was 're-verified on a second probe' that never happened. The spec's REQ-016 template bundles '(and re-verified on a second probe)' into the install-ran wording assuming the terminal fatal is only reachable after the recovery cycle, ignoring that the F19 second-launch path reaches it with a single probe. TEST-2349 only exercises the recovery path (two probes, wording honest); no test covers the F19 install-then-absent-then-upload-then-modfail terminal path, so the false claim passed the gate. Trigger is narrow (needs node-pty to break after a verified install, e.g. under concurrency) but the claim is provably wrong when it fires.
fix: Distinguish the two ways terminalNodePtyFatal is reached: only assert 're-verified on a second probe' when a second probe actually ran (thread a probedAgain/reProbed flag from runRecoveryCycle vs the canRecover=false path). On the F19 no-recovery path, state that an install was applied this connect but the agent still could not load node-pty on the retry launch — without claiming a second probe. Amend the REQ-016 template so the '(and re-verified on a second probe)' clause is conditioned on the recovery cycle having run, and add a test for the F19 install->absent->upload->module-fail terminal path asserting the diagnostic does NOT claim a second probe.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md per human (Kevin) decision 2026-07-06 — non-blocking MEDIUM/LOW robustness refinement, out of scope for this fix pass, tracked for a future feature.

claim: src/remote-client/bootstrap.ts:22's module-level doc comment still names 'coProvisionNodePty' as the entry point for the node-pty co-provisioning gate ('...runs BEFORE the sequence above -- see coProvisionNodePty'), but no function of that name exists anywhere in the file (or the repo) any more -- the ESC-003/ESC-004 loopback fixes split/renamed it into runCoProvisionPass (plus runNodePtyProbe/runNodePtyInstall). Several already-resolved findings on this ledger (FINDING-002, 003, 022, 023, 024) also cite 'coProvisionNodePty' as the symbol under discussion, reflecting the name at the time they were filed -- the top-of-file doc comment was simply never updated to track the rename, so a maintainer grepping the codebase for coProvisionNodePty (as directed by this file's own header) finds nothing.
fix: Update the doc comment at bootstrap.ts:19-23 to name the current entry point, runCoProvisionPass (in src/remote-client/bootstrap.ts), instead of the retired coProvisionNodePty name.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking LOW/MEDIUM cosmetic/quality, tracked for a future feature.

claim: The two new node-pty exec-channel spawn-failure diagnostics in bootstrap.ts are inconsistent with the two pre-existing F19 ones in the same file. connectAgent's and provisionAgent's 'failed to spawn' diagnostics (bootstrap.ts:192, :314) both name which ssh program was attempted: 'failed to spawn the ssh program "${opts.ssh?.program ?? DEFAULT_SSH_PROGRAM}"...'. The two new node-pty co-provisioning channels added by this feature, runNodePtyProbe (bootstrap.ts:532) and runNodePtyInstall (bootstrap.ts:600), hit the identical child.on('error', ...) ENOENT-class failure mode but drop the program name entirely: 'failed to spawn the ssh program for the node-pty probe: ${errText(e)}' / '...for the node-pty install: ...' -- an operator debugging a spawn failure on the new probe/install channels gets strictly less actionable information (no program name/path) than the exact same failure on the pre-existing launch/upload channels, for no stated reason. No test pins or exercises this wording (grep for the two new strings across tests/ returns nothing), so the inconsistency is invisible to the suite.
fix: Include the same ${opts.ssh?.program ?? DEFAULT_SSH_PROGRAM} program-name interpolation in the runNodePtyProbe and runNodePtyInstall spawn-failure diagnostics, matching connectAgent/provisionAgent's wording, and add a test asserting the program name appears in at least one of the two new diagnostics.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking LOW/MEDIUM cosmetic/quality, tracked for a future feature.

claim: bootstrap.ts defines two near-identical recursive bundle-dir walkers back to back: walkBundleDir (line 356-367, returns {path, bytes}[]) and walkBundleDirPaths (line 371-382, returns string[]) -- the exact same readdirSync/statSync/recurse-with-a-slash-joined-prefix algorithm, differing only in whether readFileSync is called per leaf. walkBundleDirPaths is trivially expressible as walkBundleDir(dir).map(f => f.path) (or, to avoid needlessly reading bytes just for a path-parity check, walkBundleDir could itself be reimplemented in terms of a shared path-walker that optionally reads bytes) -- instead the whole traversal loop (readdirSync, the join, the isDirectory branch, the prefix accumulation) is duplicated verbatim in the same file. A future fix to the walk semantics (e.g. symlink handling, hidden-file exclusion, or the FINDING-008-proposed .sort() for determinism) requires patching both copies correctly, and only one is currently exercised by the sha-parity path (validateManifestFiles) while the other backs the actual upload payload -- a future edit to just one copy would silently desync what gets validated from what gets uploaded.
fix: Collapse to one recursive walker, e.g. walkBundleDir(dir) returns {path, bytes}[] as today, and walkBundleDirPaths(dir) becomes walkBundleDir(dir).map(f => f.path) (or, if avoiding the byte reads for the parity check matters, factor out a shared walkPaths(dir): string[] that both walkBundleDir and walkBundleDirPaths build on) so there is exactly one traversal algorithm in this file.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking LOW/MEDIUM cosmetic/quality, tracked for a future feature.

claim: The co-provisioned node-pty is installed to an UNVERSIONED shared path <remoteAgentDir>/node_modules/node-pty (baked into PROBE_SRC/UNPACK_SRC via path.join(agentDir,'node_modules','node-pty',...) and fixed by the spec Definitions 'Remote install dir'), whereas the agent artifact is DELIBERATELY version-scoped as termhalla-agent-<version>.cjs precisely so 'an upload for version V can never overwrite the artifact a running version-W agent was launched from' (ssh-command.ts:102-104 / REQ-008). That coexistence guarantee is not extended to node-pty. Two Termhalla app versions that pin DIFFERENT node-pty versions but share the same remote host+agentDir (the default ~/.termhalla/agent, un-scoped) each read the OTHER's marker as a mismatch (manifestsMatch compares nodePtyVersion/target/ptyNodeSha256) and clean-reinstall (decideNodePtyProvision -> install, wholesale replace), so every connect from either app re-uploads the full node-pty payload -- perpetual reinstall thrash that never reaches REQ-012's promised 'idempotent steady state' (zero upload) for either version, and each replace swaps the node-pty binary a subsequently-relaunched version-W agent will import, reintroducing the exact cross-version overwrite hazard the .cjs file-name versioning was engineered to avoid. No test exercises two DIFFERENT node-pty versions against the same agentDir (the flow test pins a single nodePtyVersion:'0.0.0-stub').
fix: Version/content-scope the node-pty install the way the agent artifact is scoped -- e.g. install into a per-nodePtyVersion remote path (or fold the pinned node-pty version into the install-dir name) and point the agent's bare-specifier resolution at that path, so two app versions with different pinned node-pty versions coexist without thrash and without overwriting a running version's dependency; OR explicitly document in the spec/feature doc that a single remote agentDir is single-app-version by contract and that mixed-version sharing intentionally thrashes (and add a test asserting the two-different-versions-same-agentDir behavior is the intended one). propose CONV: when a co-provisioned remote dependency is version-locked to an artifact that is itself version-scoped on the remote to permit multi-version coexistence, the dependency's remote install path MUST be version/content-scoped too -- never share one unversioned mutable path across app versions.
location: src/remote-client/prebuilt.ts:175,189,237 (unversioned node_modules/node-pty in PROBE_SRC/UNPACK_SRC); spec Definitions 'Remote install dir'; REQ-012 / contrast ssh-command.ts:102-111 (versioned agent .cjs)
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking LOW/MEDIUM cosmetic/quality, tracked for a future feature.

claim: The exit-94 client diagnostic in bootstrap.ts (runNodePtyInstall) hardcodes the wording a sha-256 checksum mismatch was detected verifying the received pty.node, but the ESC-003/FINDING-005 loopback generalized sentinel 94 to mean a sha-256 mismatch on ANY received file (package.json or any lib/**/*.js), and UNPACK_SRC correctly names the specific failing file in its stderr (e.g. sha-256 checksum mismatch for lib/index.js). So a lib/*.js integrity failure is reported to the user with a PRIMARY message that falsely names pty.node while the real file appears only in the appended stderr tail — the two halves of the same diagnostic contradict each other (a CONV-001 which-input accuracy defect, in a security-integrity path). No flow test catches this: TEST-2348 asserts only /sha|checksum/i and its npty-corrupt rig flips the pty.node region (so the pty.node wording is coincidentally correct), and TEST-2371 (the lib-file 94 case) is unpacker-level, asserting the REMOTE stderr, never the client diagnostic.
fix: Make the 94 client diagnostic file-agnostic (e.g. a sha-256 checksum mismatch was detected verifying a received node-pty file) so it never contradicts the specific file named in the appended stderr; add a flow-level vector that corrupts a lib/*.js entry (not pty.node) and asserts the client diagnostic does not falsely name pty.node.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking LOW/MEDIUM cosmetic/quality, tracked for a future feature.

claim: resolveLaunchFatal is invoked for EVERY agent-launch fatal in connectWithProvisioning, INCLUDING the legacy path where opts.nodePty is absent (useNodePty=false, engagement=undefined). It unconditionally calls withGlibcHint, which appends the node-pty-0023 glibc-floor hint (text that literally references this build shipped node-pty prebuilt floor 2.31 and manually install the pinned node-pty version) whenever the launch stderr matches GLIBC_x.y not found. In the legacy flow no prebuilt is ever co-provisioned, so this is (a) a behavior change versus the pre-0023 flow — REQ-018 MUST requires option-absent results to be IDENTICAL to today — and (b) potentially misleading, advertising a prebuilt the legacy flow never ships. It is production-unreachable because services.ts always wires nodePty, but the remote-client is a documented headless library; any consumer that omits nodePty (or the frozen suites, had they rigged a GLIBC launch stderr) would observe the divergence. No test exercises legacy + GLIBC stderr, so REQ-018 IDENTICAL is verified only against suites that never trip the hint — the exact case the acceptance ignores.
fix: Guard resolveLaunchFatal node-pty-specific decoration (the glibc hint and the module-resolution recovery branch) behind useNodePty/engagement so the legacy (nodePty-absent) path returns the launch fatal exactly as the pre-0023 flow did; add a legacy-flow + GLIBC-stderr test asserting the diagnostic carries no glibc hint (byte-identical to the pre-0023 result).
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking LOW/MEDIUM cosmetic/quality, tracked for a future feature.

claim: The glibc-floor value is hardcoded independently in two places with no mechanical link: GLIBC_FLOOR = 2.31 in prebuilt.ts (baked into the user-facing glibcFloorHint text) and image: ubuntu:20.04 (documented glibc 2.31) in release.yml, pinned as the literal 2.31 by TEST-2311. Concept D2 and the spec Definitions explicitly document lowering the floor to 2.28 via manylinux_2_28 as an additive fast-follow. When that fast-follow (or any build-base bump, e.g. ubuntu-22.04 = glibc 2.35) changes the release container, nothing forces the prebuilt.ts hint literal to change with it, so the user-facing diagnostic would state a floor that no longer matches the actually-shipped binary — telling an operator their host is older than 2.31 when the real floor is different. This is the CONV-058-class drift (a version literal fixed at authoring time instead of derived from the producer output) applied to the glibc floor; no test cross-checks the two 2.31 literals against each other.
fix: Derive the floor in the hint from a single source shared with (or cross-checked against) the release container base, or add a structural test tying prebuilt.ts GLIBC_FLOOR to the release.yml container literal so a future floor change cannot desync the hint from the shipped binary. propose CONV: a user-facing floor/version literal that describes a build-produced artifact (glibc floor, N-API/Node floor, target triple) MUST be derived from or cross-checked against the single build-time source that produced the artifact, never independently hardcoded in the client message.
resolution: Deferred to docs/superpowers/0023-remote-node-pty-prebuilt-review-followups.md — approved at human-review by Kevin 2026-07-06; non-blocking LOW/MEDIUM cosmetic/quality, tracked for a future feature.
